title: Observable — The Immune Checkpoint Cancer Hijacks
logline: PD-1 meets PD-L1 — the molecular brake that healthy cells use and tumors exploit, and the contact that checkpoint-blockade drugs break. Structure solved at 2.0 Å (PDB 4ZQK).

brand.title: Observable
brand.subtitle: Upon Tyne Science — evidence before wonder
brand.cta: Follow Observable — one measurement at a time.

science_subject.key_measurement: Human PD-1:PD-L1 complex solved by X-ray crystallography at 2.0 Å (PDB 4ZQK, Zak et al. 2015); blocking this contact is the basis of anti-PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint immunotherapy (2018 Nobel Prize, Allison & Honjo)
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific molecular visualization, immune checkpoint synapse — PD-1 receptor on a T-cell lipid bilayer bound to PD-L1 ligand on an opposing tumor-cell bilayer across a thin aqueous gap, interface geometry per PDB 4ZQK, neighboring membrane proteins and surface glycans showing molecular crowding, faint solvent, neutral dark background, accurate relative scale, no anthropomorphic faces, no directed lunging, reversible probabilistic contact, Jantzen molecular-cellular fidelity, 16:9, ILLUSTRATIVE NOT TO SCALE label lower corner

sources.citation: Zak KM, Kitel R, Przetocka S, et al. Structure of the Complex of Human Programmed Death 1, PD-1, and Its Ligand PD-L1. Structure. 2015;23(12):2341-2348 (PDB 4ZQK, 2.0 Å)
sources.notes: X-ray structure of the human PD-1/PD-L1 binding interface — CC0 coordinates

sources.citation: Sharpe AH, Pauken KE. The diverse functions of the PD1 inhibitory pathway. Nat Rev Immunol. 2018;18(3):153-167
sources.notes: Peer-reviewed review of PD-1/PD-L1 biology and checkpoint blockade

beats.speech_text: Your immune system can recognize and kill a cancer cell. So why doesn't it always? Often, the tumor has found the off-switch — and it's a single molecular handshake.

It's called an immune checkpoint. A T cell carries a receptor named PD-1. When PD-1 touches a partner protein, PD-L1, on a neighboring cell, it dials the T cell down — a built-in brake that normally stops your immune system attacking healthy tissue. Many tumors simply coat themselves in PD-L1 and press that brake.

In twenty fifteen, crystallographers resolved the human PD-1 to PD-L1 contact at two angstroms — coordinates anyone can download. And we know the brake is real because releasing it works: antibodies that block this exact handshake unleash the immune system on tumors, a breakthrough recognized with the twenty-eighteen Nobel Prize in Medicine.

Here is that handshake — PD-1 on one membrane, PD-L1 on the other, meeting across a crowded molecular gap. This is a structural model, not a microscope photograph, and it's a reversible, chance encounter — no molecule is hunting another.
beats.visualization_prompt: PD-1/PD-L1 interface per PDB 4ZQK between two apposed lipid bilayers, crowded membrane proteins and glycans, reversible contact, no faces, Jantzen molecular-cellular fidelity

beats.speech_text: One small contact between two proteins decides whether your immune system fights or stands down — and learning to block it rewrote cancer treatment. The molecules don't intend any of it; the consequences are enormous.

lines: EVIDENCE: Human PD-1:PD-L1 complex, PDB 4ZQK (Zak et al., Structure 2015), 2.0 Å
VIZ: Illustrative structural model — labeled NOT TO SCALE; not a micrograph; Jantzen molecular fidelity
